title: Family Dinner

story.scene: Carlos and Marta host a relaxed family dinner in Madrid. Ana joins you, and Carlos’s parents and siblings are visiting from Granada.
story.mission: Today you’ll meet Carlos’s family and learn to introduce the people closest to you.
story.continuity: You greet Ana again and reuse names, origin, and residence while meeting the family Carlos mentioned at the end of Lesson 2.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner is arriving as a guest among unfamiliar relatives.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner feels welcomed into Carlos’s family circle.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Meeting the family and sharing who matters in their own life.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Mañana te escribo. Quiero enseñarte otro lugar especial de Madrid.
microCliffhanger.english: I’ll message you tomorrow. I want to show you another special place in Madrid.

carlosIntroduction.title: Meet my family
carlosIntroduction.message: ¡Hola! Ana is here, and my family is visiting from Granada. I’d love you to meet everyone. Listen as I introduce them, then tell me about the people in your family. No long list—just a real family dinner.
carlosIntroduction.prompt: Start by saying: Hola, Carlos. ¿Cómo estás?

canDo: I can introduce people in my family.
I can ask someone about their family.
I can say who a person is.
I can answer basic questions about family.
objectives: Meet six members of Carlos’s family through a real-life story.
Use mi, tu, and su to identify family relationships.
Ask and answer simple questions about family.
Complete a short family conversation with Carlos.

essentialPhrases.usage: Use esta es to introduce a woman or girl.
Use este es to introduce a man or boy.
Ask this when you want to identify a woman or girl.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Who is she? She’s my sister.

essentialPhrases.usage: Ask this when you want to identify a man or boy.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Who is he? He’s my brother.

essentialPhrases.usage: A natural way to ask whether someone has brothers or sisters.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Do you have siblings? Yes, I have a sister.

essentialPhrases.usage: Use tengo + a family member to answer questions about your family.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Yes, I have a sister. Her name is Sofía.

essentialPhrases.usage: A complete and natural answer if you have no brothers or sisters.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: No, I don’t have siblings. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Use mi familia when talking about your family.
Use esta es before introducing a woman or girl.
Use este es before introducing a man or boy.
Quién carries an accent in a direct question.
The accent distinguishes él, he, from el, the.
Use ella to refer to a woman or girl.
Mamá is warm and common in everyday family conversation.
Papá is warm and common in everyday family conversation.
The h is silent.
Hermanos can mean brothers or siblings, depending on context.
Use hermanos for siblings when the group is mixed or unspecified.
Use mi esposa for my wife.
Use mi esposo for my husband.
Pareja is a broadly useful and inclusive word for partner.
The h is silent.
The h is silent.
Hijos often means children, not only sons.
Padres means parents; parientes means relatives.
Abuela is grandmother; abuelita is an affectionate form.
Abuelo is grandfather; abuelito is an affectionate form.
Keep the written accent on í.
Keep the written accent on í.
The ending changes with the person: prima or primo.
Mi stays the same for masculine and feminine singular nouns.
Tu has no accent when it means your.
Context tells you whether su means his or her in this lesson.
Use nuestro with a masculine noun and nuestra with a feminine noun.
Learn tengo as a complete chunk for answering family questions.

grammar.topic: Possessive adjectives: mi, tu, su, and nuestro/nuestra
grammar.meaning: Show whose family member you are talking about.
grammar.explanation: Put a possessive word directly before the family noun: mi madre, tu padre, su hermana. Use nuestro or nuestra for our, matching the family word: nuestro hijo, nuestra hija. The possessive tells whose relationship it is; it does not change for the person’s gender except nuestro/nuestra.

notes.title: My and your
notes.text: Use mi for my and tu for your: mi hermano, tu hermana. Both work with masculine and feminine singular family words.

notes.title: His or her
notes.text: Use su for his or her: Carlos y su madre; Ana y su padre. The conversation tells you whose family member you mean.

notes.title: Our
notes.text: Use nuestro before a masculine singular word and nuestra before a feminine singular word: nuestro hijo, nuestra hija.

notes.title: Keep the word before the relationship
notes.text: Spanish says mi madre, not madre mi. Keep mi, tu, su, or nuestro/nuestra directly before the family noun.

examples.english: Carlos is talking with his sister.

grammar.practicePrompt: Introduce two real or imagined family members using mi, then ask Carlos one question using tu.

dialogue.title: Dinner with Carlos’s family
dialogue.setting: Carlos opens the door and introduces you to his family before dinner.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.spanish: ¡Hola de nuevo! Mucho gusto en verte.
lines.english: Hello again! It’s nice to see you.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Muy bien. ¡Bienvenido! Esta es mi madre, Elena.
lines.english: Very well. Welcome! This is my mother, Elena.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, Elena. Me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto.
lines.english: Hi, Elena. My name is Tom. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Elena
lines.spanish: Mucho gusto, Tom. Soy de Granada.
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Tom. I’m from Granada.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Este es mi padre, Javier. Ella es mi hermana, Lucía, y él es mi hermano, Diego.
lines.english: This is my father, Javier. She is my sister, Lucía, and he is my brother, Diego.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Nice to meet you. Carlos, who is she?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Ella es mi esposa, Marta. Y este es nuestro hijo, Nico.
lines.english: She is my wife, Marta. And this is our son, Nico.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: And you? Do you have siblings?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
lines.english: Yes, I have a sister. Her name is Sofía.

pronunciation.focus: Silent h and smooth possessive-family chunks
tips: The h is silent in hermano, hermana, hijo, and hija.
Keep mi madre and tu padre connected as short thought groups.
Do not add an English h sound before hermano or hermana.
In nuestra familia, keep every vowel clear and light.

items.note: Keep both words short and even.
The h is silent; begin hermano with the vowel sound.
Link su hermana without adding an h sound.
The h in hijo is silent.

listening.title: Can you follow the family dinner?
listening.instructions: Listen, follow along, then check what you understood.
listening.naturalScript: Hola, Carlos. ¿Cómo estás? Muy bien. ¡Bienvenido! Esta es mi madre, Elena. Hola, Elena. Me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto. Mucho gusto, Tom. Soy de Granada. Este es mi padre, Javier. Ella es mi hermana, Lucía, y él es mi hermano, Diego. Carlos, ¿quién es ella? Ella es mi esposa, Marta. Y este es nuestro hijo, Nico. ¿Y tú? ¿Tienes hermanos? Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía. ¡Qué bien! Vamos a cenar.
listening.slowScript: Hola, Carlos. / ¿Cómo estás? / Muy bien. / ¡Bienvenido! / Esta es mi madre, Elena. / Hola, Elena. / Me llamo Tom. / Mucho gusto. / Mucho gusto, Tom. / Soy de Granada. / Este es mi padre, Javier. / Ella es mi hermana, Lucía, / y él es mi hermano, Diego. / Carlos, ¿quién es ella? / Ella es mi esposa, Marta. / Y este es nuestro hijo, Nico. / ¿Y tú? / ¿Tienes hermanos? / Sí, tengo una hermana. / Se llama Sofía. / ¡Qué bien! / Vamos a cenar.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Muy bien. ¡Bienvenido! Esta es mi madre, Elena.
transcript.english: Very well. Welcome! This is my mother, Elena.

transcript.speaker: Learner y Elena
transcript.spanish: Hola, Elena. Me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto. Mucho gusto, Tom. Soy de Granada.
transcript.english: Hi, Elena. My name is Tom. Nice to meet you. Nice to meet you, Tom. I’m from Granada.

transcript.speaker: Carlos
transcript.spanish: Este es mi padre, Javier. Ella es mi hermana, Lucía, y él es mi hermano, Diego.
transcript.english: This is my father, Javier. She is my sister, Lucía, and he is my brother, Diego.

transcript.speaker: Learner y Carlos
transcript.spanish: Carlos, ¿quién es ella? Ella es mi esposa, Marta. Y este es nuestro hijo, Nico.
transcript.english: Carlos, who is she? She is my wife, Marta. And this is our son, Nico.

transcript.speaker: Carlos y Learner
transcript.spanish: ¿Y tú? ¿Tienes hermanos? Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
transcript.english: And you? Do you have siblings? Yes, I have a sister. Her name is Sofía.

comprehension.explanation: Carlos first says “Esta es mi madre, Elena.”
Carlos says “este es nuestro hijo, Nico.”

comprehension.prompt: What does Carlos ask the learner?
comprehension.explanation: ¿Tienes hermanos? asks whether someone has siblings.

listeningPhrases.note: Listen for mi directly before madre.
The h in hermano is silent.
Context tells you that su means his here.

listeningPhrases.english: Do you have siblings? Yes, I have a sister.
listeningPhrases.note: Hear the question and its complete answer as one exchange.

speaking.instructions: Carlos guides you through the family dinner one response at a time.

items.label: Listen
items.cue: Listen once, then introduce the same person.

items.label: Your turn
items.cue: Say the introduction back in one smooth phrase.
items.focus: Keep the h in hermano silent.

items.label: Keep talking
items.prompt: Carlos points to Lucía and asks: ¿Quién es ella?
items.cue: Identify Lucía using tu because you are speaking to Carlos.
items.focus: Use tu because you are speaking to Carlos.

items.label: One more challenge
items.prompt: Ask Carlos whether he has siblings.
items.cue: Try the question from memory before opening the model.
items.focus: Let your voice rise naturally at the end.

items.label: Finish the conversation
items.prompt: Give a true answer, or use the model if you prefer not to share personal details.
items.cue: Share one true detail or use the imagined-family model.
items.text: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
items.meaning: Yes, I have a sister. Her name is Sofía.
items.focus: Say the two short sentences confidently without rushing.

miniConversation.setting: You and Ana are seated at dinner with Carlos and his family.
miniConversation.goal: Identify two people, ask Carlos about his family, and share one detail about yours.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: ¡Hola! ¿Cómo estás? Esta es mi madre, Elena.
turns.english: Hi! How are you? This is my mother, Elena.

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Greet Elena, say your name, and say mucho gusto.
turns.exampleSpanish: Hola, Elena. Me llamo Tom. Mucho gusto.
turns.exampleEnglish: Hi, Elena. My name is Tom. Nice to meet you.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.english: Nice to meet you. Who is he?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Give a true answer or use an imagined family. Add a name if you can.
turns.exampleSpanish: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
turns.exampleEnglish: Yes, I have a sister. Her name is Sofía.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: ¡Qué bien! Gracias por hablar de tu familia.
turns.english: That’s great! Thanks for talking about your family.

culture.title: Meeting family in Spain
culture.text: Family gatherings are often warm and conversational. When meeting relatives, mucho gusto is always safe. Friends and family may greet with a hug or cheek kisses, while a handshake is also perfectly appropriate if you are unsure—follow the other person’s lead. Spanish names may include two surnames, one from each parent, but people usually use their first name in friendly introductions.

nativeSpeech.title: Family words you will hear naturally
notes: Mamá and papá are warmer everyday choices than madre and padre when speaking about your own parents.
Hermanos can mean brothers or siblings when the group is mixed or unspecified.
Hijos often means children, not only sons.
Pareja is a common and inclusive word for partner.

commonMistakes.explanation: The possessive comes directly before the family word.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using mí with an accent for my.
commonMistakes.correction: Say mi madre, without an accent on mi.
commonMistakes.explanation: Mi means my. Mí with an accent is used after a preposition and is not the word you need here.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying su madre when speaking directly to Carlos about Carlos’s mother.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use tu for your when speaking directly to someone. Use su when speaking about his or her family.

commonMistakes.correction: Begin hermano with the vowel sound: er-MA-no.

canDo: I can introduce close family members.
I can identify someone using él or ella.
I can ask ¿tienes hermanos? and give a basic answer.
I can use mi, tu, su, and nuestro/nuestra in useful family phrases.
review.conversationReturn: The next time Carlos asks how you are, add one family detail: Estoy bien. Mi familia está en casa.

quiz.explanation: Mi means my, and madre means mother.

quiz.prompt: Choose the Spanish for my brother.
quiz.explanation: Mi means my, and hermano means brother.
Hermana means sister; hermano means brother.
Padres means parents. Parientes means relatives.

quiz.prompt: Carlos points to his sister. Which sentence is correct?
quiz.explanation: Ella means she, and hermana means sister.

quiz.prompt: You are speaking directly to Carlos. How do you say your mother?
quiz.explanation: Use tu for your when speaking directly to someone.

quiz.prompt: You are talking about Ana and her father. Complete: Ana y _____ padre.
quiz.explanation: Su means her in this context.

quiz.prompt: Choose the correct phrase for our daughter.
quiz.explanation: Use nuestra before the feminine singular noun hija.

quiz.prompt: What is the best answer to ¿Tienes hermanos?
quiz.explanation: The question asks whether you have siblings.

quiz.prompt: Which question means Who is she?
quiz.explanation: Quién asks who, and ella means she.
Mi means my and comes before madre.
Tu means your when speaking directly to someone.

quiz.prompt: Carlos says: Esta es mi esposa, Marta. Who is Marta?
You point to your father. Which introduction is natural?
quiz.explanation: Use este es for a man and mi for your own relationship.

quiz.prompt: Carlos and Marta introduce their son. What should they say?
quiz.explanation: Nuestro agrees with the masculine singular noun hijo.
Ella means she, and mi hermana means my sister.
Quién means who, and él means he.

quiz.prompt: In the model conversation, Carlos asks: ¿Tienes hermanos? Which response directly answers and adds one detail?
options: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
Mi amiga Ana vive en Madrid.
Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg.
quiz.answer: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.
quiz.explanation: Sí, tengo una hermana directly answers the sibling question, and Se llama Sofía adds one clear detail. Ana remains Carlos’s friend.

flashcards.selectionRule: Only Tier 1 vocabulary and the most useful relationship chunks appear in the core deck.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce one woman in your family.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Esta es mi madre. Se llama Elena.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Introduce one man in your family.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Este es mi hermano. Se llama Diego.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Answer Carlos with a true or imagined family detail.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Sí, tengo una hermana. Se llama Sofía.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Use mi, tu, and su in three short family phrases.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Mi madre. Tu padre. Su hermana.

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Family Dinner
realLifeMission.mission: At dinner, greet Carlos’s family, identify two people, ask Carlos about one family member, and share two simple details about your own or an imagined family.
successCriteria: Reuse hola, me llamo, ¿cómo estás?, soy de, or mucho gusto naturally.
Introduce or identify at least two people with mi, tu, or su.
Answer with tengo + a family member or no tengo hermanos.
Say one family member’s name with se llama.
Respond to one natural follow-up from Carlos.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Excelente! You met my family and told me about yours. That was a real family conversation.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the message first. Correct only one possessive or family-word error that affects meaning.
realLifeMission.handoff: Mañana te escribo. Quiero enseñarte otro lugar especial de Madrid.

carlosClosing: You met my family and told me about yours entirely in Spanish. Everyone was happy you joined us. Tomorrow I’ll send you a message so we can make our next plan.

passportStamp.title: Family Dinner
passportStamp.description: You met Carlos’s family and shared who matters to you.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 3.

achievement.title: Family Friend
achievement.description: Introduce family members and complete your first family conversation.
